value.market_read: 3-2-1 Crack: The 3-2-1 settled at $56.68/bbl, down $4.93 w/w — a sharp pullback from the prior week's $61.61 print. The move is a mechanical unwind against crude that fell only modestly: WTI settled at $70.10, down $3.11 on the week, so with product settlements sliding faster than crude, the blended crack compressed. Yesterday's WPSR (week ending June 19) showed a 6.09 mb crude draw against a 2.06 mb gasoline build and 3.06 mb distillate build, so the product-side supply length capped upside even as crude tightened. Watch: Whether refinery utilization holding at 96.1% keeps product output ahead of demand and pressures the crack further into July. Gasoline Crack: The RBOB crack settled at $52.13/bbl, off $5.70 w/w — the weakest section this week. The 2.06 mb gasoline build reported in the June 19 WPSR undercut the summer-driving narrative and drove the gasoline-led leg lower, with product weakness outpacing the $3.11 WTI decline. In a distillate-led regime, gasoline is the softer of the two barrels, and this week's build confirmed that positioning. Watch: Post-holiday gasoline draws — a build during peak driving season is a bearish tell if it repeats. Distillate Crack: The ULSD crack settled at $65.78/bbl, the firmest of the product cracks and consistent with the distillate-led regime. Despite a 3.06 mb distillate build in the June 19 report, the diesel crack held its premium to gasoline, reflecting structurally tight middle-distillate balances and firm freight/trucking pull. Diesel continues to carry the barrel here even against seasonal gasoline strength. Watch: Whether distillate builds accumulate through summer, which would erode the crack ahead of the winter heating-demand ramp. Jet Crack: The jet crack settled at $34.96/bbl, the lowest-value cut of the slate. Summer air-travel demand is supportive, but refiners weighing regrade economics favor diesel yield when the distillate crack sits at $65.78 versus jet at $34.96 — a wide spread that pulls barrels toward ULSD. Jet remains the residual middle-distillate outlet this week. Watch: Regrade economics — if the diesel-jet spread stays this wide, expect jet yields trimmed in favor of ULSD. Brent–WTI Spread: Brent–WTI settled at $3.34/bbl, a narrow spread that signals a weak US export arb. Yet the data shows crude exports running 4.67 mb/d with ULSD at 1.39 mb/d — still firm — so barrels are moving even with the tight arb; the 6.09 mb crude draw reflects that pull plus strong 96.1% runs. With WTI down $3.11 w/w and Brent softer alongside, the spread stayed compressed rather than re-widening. Watch: The arb needs WTI to fall (or Brent to rise) to re-widen toward $5+ and re-incentivize incremental export flow.
value.chart_321: At $56.68/bbl the 3-2-1 sits comfortably in the upper half of the 5-yr band, but the $4.93 w/w drop marks a clear turn lower after the run to $61.61. The four-week trajectory — climbing from the mid-40s in late May to a $61.61 peak before this pullback — shows the summer margin build cresting. The retracement is product-led, not crude-led, with the June 19 gasoline and distillate builds capping the top.
value.chart_gas: The gasoline crack's $5.70 w/w slide to $52.13/bbl is the sharpest single-cut move this week, and the 2.06 mb gasoline build behind it undercuts the expected Memorial Day-through-July driving pull. In a distillate-led regime gasoline is already the weaker leg, and a build during peak season signals demand is not clearing summer-spec supply as aggressively as runs at 96.1% imply. Watch for draws to resume before calling the gasoline top confirmed.
value.chart_dist: At $65.78/bbl the distillate crack anchors the slate and defines the distillate-led regime, holding a wide premium over gasoline at $52.13. Firm trucking and freight diesel demand plus structurally tight middle-distillate balances keep ULSD margins elevated even against a 3.06 mb weekly build. The summer setup is unusual — distillate typically firms into winter — so current strength reflects tight balances rather than seasonal heating pull.
value.chart_jet: The jet crack at $34.96/bbl is the weakest cut, sitting well below diesel despite supportive summer air-travel demand. The $30+ gap to ULSD makes regrade economics decisively favor diesel yield, pulling barrels away from jet at the margin. Jet remains the swing middle-distillate outlet, and its discount to diesel is the key yield-decision variable this week.
value.chart_bw: Brent–WTI at $3.34/bbl is narrow, reflecting WTI's relative firmness rather than a wide export-arb window. Even so, crude exports at 4.67 mb/d and ULSD exports at 1.39 mb/d show flows remain competitive, and the 6.09 mb crude draw ties to that pull plus 96.1% runs. To re-widen the arb and restore stronger incremental export economics, WTI would need to fall or Brent to rise — the current compression is not a WTI-upside signal.
